_meta.coordinate_source: Wikipedia, Survey of India, Archaeological Survey of India, government tourism boards
_meta.notes: Temple sequences follow traditional/scriptural order. Distances are approximate road distances. Some temples appear in multiple circuits. All circuits reference temple IDs in mandirs.json.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Himalayan Char Dham Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.season_open: April/May to October/November (temples closed in winter)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate to Strenuous (high altitude, treks required for 2 of 4 dhams)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The most important pilgrimage in Uttarakhand (DevBhumi — Land of Gods). Conceived by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century. Over 3 million pilgrims annually. Route runs west to east following traditional clockwise direction.
pilgrimage_circuits.permit_info: Yatra registration mandatory from 2022. Online via uttarakhandtourism.gov.in or district registrations.

temples.name: Yamunotri Temple
temples.significance: Origin of river Yamuna. First dham on the circuit. Hot spring (Surya Kund) where pilgrims cook rice and potatoes to offer to the deity.

temples.name: Gangotri Temple
temples.significance: Origin of River Ganga. Most accessible dham — road reaches temple directly. Gaumukh glacier (actual source) is 18 km further.

temples.name: Kedarnath Temple
temples.significance: Highest Jyotirlinga at 3,583 m. One of the Panch Kedars. The only Jyotirlinga in the Char Dham circuit. Temple dates to 8th century (rebuilt by Adi Shankaracharya).

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.significance: Final and most important dham. Also part of Maha Char Dham and Panch Badri. Directly accessible by road. Tapt Kund (hot spring) at entrance used for ritual bathing.
nearby_attractions: Mana Village (last Indian village before Tibet)

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Maha Char Dham (Pan-India)
pilgrimage_circuits.deity_focus: Multi-deity (Vishnu/Shiva at four cardinal directions of India)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Easy to Moderate (all accessible by road or air)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The original Char Dham established by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century to unite India spiritually. Pilgrims visit one temple at each cardinal direction: North (Badrinath), East (Puri), South (Rameswaram), West (Dwarka). Completing all four is believed to grant moksha.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Badrinath → Dwarka → Rameswaram → Puri (or any order, flexible)

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.significance: Northern dham. Part of Himalayan Char Dham. One of 108 Divya Desams.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
temples.significance: Western dham. Legendary capital of Lord Krishna. Located at land's end in Gujarat.

temples.name: Ramanathaswamy Temple
temples.significance: Southern dham. Located on Pamban Island. World's longest temple corridor (1,200 m). Also a Jyotirlinga.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple
temples.significance: Eastern dham. Famous for world-renowned Rath Yatra chariot festival.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: 12 Jyotirlinga Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The 12 abodes of Shiva as radiant columns of divine light (Jyoti). Mentioned in the Shiva Purana and the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra. Completing all 12 is believed to wash away all sins and grant moksha. Traditional clockwise order shown below.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Somnath → Mallikarjuna → Mahakaleshwar → Omkareshwar → Kedarnath → Bhimashankar → Kashi Vishwanath → Trimbakeshwar → Vaidyanath → Nageshwar → Ramanathaswamy → Grishneshwar

pilgrimage_circuits.name: 51 Shakti Peetha Circuit (Major India-based)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Varies — some are accessible, Vaishno Devi requires 14 km trek
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Sacred spots where body parts of Goddess Sati (Shiva's consort) fell after Lord Vishnu dismembered her body with the Sudarshana Chakra to release grief-stricken Shiva from her corpse. Each site has a presiding Shakti (goddess form) and her Bhairava (Shiva form). Considered the most powerful pilgrimage for Devi devotees.

major_peethas.name: Kamakhya Temple
major_peethas.significance: Most powerful Shakti Peetha. Womb/reproductive organ of Sati. Annual Ambubachi Mela (June) draws 500,000+ tantric sadhus and pilgrims.

major_peethas.name: Vaishno Devi Temple
major_peethas.body_part: Right arm (considered a Shakti Peetha by many traditions)

major_peethas.name: Karani Mata Temple
major_peethas.special_note: Famous for 25,000+ sacred rats (kabbas) who live inside the temple

major_peethas.name: Hinglaj Mata Temple
major_peethas.special_note: Northernmost major Shakti Peetha. Accessible for Indian pilgrims on special occasion; largest multi-faith annual gathering in Pakistan.

pilgrimage_circuits.additional_peethas_note: The full 51 Shakti Peetha circuit also includes temples in Nepal (Guhyeshwari, Manasa), Bangladesh (Sugandha, Bhavani), and Sri Lanka (Mangalore). Major Indian peethas above cover the key pilgrimage sites.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Panch Kedar Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Strenuous (multiple treks of 10–24 km, high altitude)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five sacred temples where the bull form of Lord Shiva (Nandi) disappeared underground after the Pandavas sought his blessings for penance after the Kurukshetra War. Each temple enshrines a different body part of Shiva. One of the most challenging and rewarding Himalayan pilgrimages.
pilgrimage_circuits.mythology: After the Kurukshetra war, the Pandavas sought Shiva's blessings for killing their kin. Shiva avoided them by hiding as a bull in the Garhwal Himalayas. When they found him, he dived underground — different body parts surfaced at five different locations.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Panch Badri Yatra
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate (Bhavishya Badri requires some trek; others accessible by road)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five sacred abodes of Lord Vishnu in his Badri (jujube berry) form. The Badri Kshetra (the valley of the Alaknanda river) is considered Vishnu's eternal abode. Completing all five is considered equivalent to completing all Divya Desams.

temples.name: Badri Vishal (Badrinath)
temples.significance: Main and supreme Badri. Part of both Himalayan Char Dham and Maha Char Dham.

temples.name: Bhavishya Badri Temple
temples.significance: Future abode of Badrinath — scriptures say when Joshimath's Narasimha idol loses its arm, Badrinath will shift here. Requires a 6 km trek from Tapovan.

temples.name: Vridh Badri Temple
temples.significance: Ancient form of Badrinath worshipped by sages. Lord Vishnu appeared as an old man (Vridh) here to grant blessings.

temples.name: Yogdhyan Badri Temple
temples.significance: Pandavas meditated here. Lord Vishnu is worshipped in a meditative (yogic) posture.

temples.name: Adi Badri Temple
temples.significance: Oldest and most accessible. Complex of 14 temples. Open year-round unlike Badrinath.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Sapta Puri (Seven Sacred Cities)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The seven holiest cities of Hinduism (Sapta Moksha Puri — seven cities of liberation). Visiting all seven is believed to grant moksha even without completing any specific ritual. Mentioned in the Vishnu Smriti and Garuda Purana.
pilgrimage_circuits.shloka: Ayodhyā Mathurā Māyā Kāśī Kāñcī Avantikā | Purī Dvārakā caiva saptaitā mokṣadāyikāḥ ||

cities.name: Ayodhya
cities.significance: Birthplace of Lord Rama. One of the most sacred cities in Hinduism. Now home to the newly inaugurated Ram Mandir (2024).

cities.name: Mathura
cities.significance: Birthplace of Lord Krishna. One of the holiest cities. Mathura–Vrindavan is the Braj region (Krishna's childhood home).

cities.name: Haridwar
cities.deity: Lord Vishnu (Har ki Pauri — Vishnu's footprint)
cities.primary_temple: Mansa Devi Temple & Har ki Pauri (Ghat)
cities.significance: Gateway to the four Himalayan Dhams. Kumbh Mela every 12 years. Ganga Aarti at Har ki Pauri is one of India's most iconic rituals.

cities.name: Varanasi (Kashi)
cities.significance: Oldest living city in the world. Dying in Kashi grants moksha. 84 ghats line the Ganga. The city never sleeps — Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat (daily).
other_key_sites: Sarnath (5 km — where Buddha gave first sermon)

cities.name: Kanchipuram
cities.significance: City of 1,000 temples (108 Shaivite, 108 Vaishnavite). One of the seven Mukti Kshetras. Headquarters of Shankaracharya for South India. Famous for silk weaving.

cities.name: Ujjain (Avantika)
cities.significance: Ancient city on the Tropic of Cancer. Ujjain was once the prime meridian of ancient India. Kumbh Mela (Simhastha) every 12 years. Home of Mahakaleshwar Jyotirlinga.

cities.name: Dwarka
cities.significance: Legendary capital of Lord Krishna. Considered the westernmost Char Dham. The ancient city is believed to be submerged in the Arabian Sea (Dwarka Nagari).

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Ashtavinayak Yatra (Maharashtra)
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Easy (all motorable, within 200 km of Pune)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Eight self-manifested (Swayambhu) Ganesha temples within a 300 km radius of Pune. Circuit begins and ends at Moreshwar (Morgaon). Completing the circuit is believed to fulfil all desires and cleanse sins. Traditional anticlockwise direction around Pune.
pilgrimage_circuits.traditional_route_order: Morgaon → Siddhatek → Pali → Mahad → Theur → Lenyadri → Ozar → Ranjangaon → (back to Morgaon)

temples.name: Moreshwar Temple (Morgaon)
temples.significance: First and last Ashtavinayak. The starting point of the circuit. Ganesha rides a peacock here.

temples.name: Siddhatek — Siddhi Vinayak Temple
temples.significance: Lord Vishnu worshipped Ganesha here to gain Siddhi (power) to defeat demons. The idol faces east (right side) — unique in the circuit.

temples.name: Pali — Ballaleshwar Temple
temples.significance: Only Ashtavinayak where Ganesha is named after a devotee (Ballal). Both Ganesha and Ballal's eyes are closed.

temples.name: Mahad — Varadavinayak Temple
temples.significance: The boon-granting Ganesha. An eternal lamp (Nandadeep) has been burning here continuously since 1892.

temples.name: Theur — Chintamani Temple
temples.significance: Ganesha retrieved the Chintamani gem from demon Guna for the sage Kapila. Peshwa Madhavrao spent his last days here. Highly revered by the Peshwa rulers.

temples.name: Lenyadri — Girijatmaj Temple
temples.significance: Only Ashtavinayak inside a cave (Buddhist caves complex). 283 steps to reach. Parvati performed penance here for 12 years to get Ganesha as her son.

temples.name: Ozar — Vighnahar Temple
temples.significance: Ganesha defeated the demon Vighasur here. The idol's trunk turns left (inauspicious, powerful form) — hence requires strict ritual compliance.

temples.name: Ranjangaon — Mahaganapati Temple
temples.significance: Shiva prayed to Ganesha here before his battle against Tripurasura. The idol has 10 trunks and 20 arms. A hidden chamber contains a larger, more ancient idol.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Pancha Bhuta Stala Circuit (Tamil Nadu)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Five ancient Shiva temples of South India, each representing one of the five classical elements (Pancha Bhutas). Mentioned in the Thevaram (Shaiva canonical text). Completing the circuit is believed to lead to liberation from the cycle of rebirth.

temples.name: Nataraja Temple (Chidambaram)
temples.significance: Shiva worshipped as formless space. The Chidambara Rahasyam (inner sanctum secret) is a curtain representing the sky — the invisible form of Shiva.

temples.name: Jambukeshwara Temple (Thiruvanaikaval)
temples.significance: The Shivalinga is always submerged in naturally-springing water inside the Garbha Griha (sanctum). The water level never drops. One of the oldest temples in Tamil Nadu.

temples.name: Arunachaleswarar Temple (Tiruvannamalai)
temples.significance: Lord Shiva manifested as a column of fire to settle a dispute between Brahma and Vishnu. The hill Arunachala IS the Shivalinga. Deepam festival lights a fire visible for miles. Sri Ramana Maharshi's ashram is here.

temples.name: Ekambareswarar Temple (Kanchipuram)
temples.significance: Shiva worshipped as Earth element. The Shivalinga is made of sand (Prithvi). An ancient mango tree with four branches represents the four Vedas.

temples.name: Kalahasteeswara Temple (Srikalahasti)
temples.significance: The only Pancha Bhuta Stala in Andhra Pradesh. The flame inside flickers constantly (showing moving air) despite no windows or vents. Famous for Rahu-Ketu Dosha (Rahukala puja) remedies.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Divya Desam South India Circuit
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: 108 sacred Vishnu temples glorified in the Tamil Prabandham by the 12 Alvars (Vaishnava poet-saints). First of the 108 is Srirangam, last is Tirupati. Completing all 108 is a lifetime spiritual goal for Vaishnavites.
pilgrimage_circuits.note: Full 108 Divya Desam itinerary is a lifetime circuit. Above lists the key major Desams. Tirupati (AP) and Srirangam (TN) are the most visited.

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Braj Parikrama (Mathura-Vrindavan Circuit)
pilgrimage_circuits.best_occasion: Kartik month (October–November) — lakhs do the parikrama on foot
pilgrimage_circuits.difficulty: Moderate (long foot pilgrimage; shorter Panchakroshi Parikrama in 5 days also popular)
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: The sacred land of Lord Krishna's childhood. The 84 Kos Braj Parikrama circumambulates the entire Braj Mandal covering 12 forests where Krishna played. Devotees walk the route barefoot, singing bhajans.

key_stops.name: Vrindavan
key_stops.significance: Krishna's childhood forest — the eternal Vrindavan

key_stops.name: Govardhan Hill
key_stops.significance: Hill lifted by Krishna to protect Braj from Indra's rains (Govardhan Puja)

key_stops.name: Barsana
key_stops.significance: Birthplace of Radha Rani. Famous for Lathmar Holi

key_stops.name: Nandagaon
key_stops.significance: Krishna's foster father Nanda Baba's village

key_stops.name: Gokul
key_stops.significance: Where baby Krishna was raised by Yashoda and Nanda

pilgrimage_circuits.name: Odisha Golden Triangle Pilgrimage
pilgrimage_circuits.deity_focus: Lord Jagannath (Vishnu), Lord Shiva, Lord Surya
pilgrimage_circuits.significance: Odisha's most important pilgrimage circuit combining the Maha Char Dham (Puri Jagannath), the UNESCO World Heritage Konark Sun Temple, the Temple City of Bhubaneswar, and the Chilika Lake. Best example of Kalinga architecture.

temples.name: Lingaraja Temple
temples.significance: Largest and finest temple of Bhubaneswar's 700+ temples. 11th century Kalinga architecture.

temples.name: Mukteshwar Temple
temples.significance: Gem of Odisha temple architecture (10th century). Intricate carvings. The torana (arch) at entrance is unparalleled in Indian temple art.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple (Puri)
temples.significance: Maha Char Dham. World-renowned Rath Yatra. The Mahaprasad (consecrated food) is believed to be cooked by Goddess Lakshmi herself.

temples.name: Konark Sun Temple
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage. The Black Pagoda. 13th century chariot of the Sun with 24 giant carved wheels.

temples.name: Sakshi Gopal Temple
temples.significance: Krishna bore witness (Sakshi) to a promise made by a devotee — a living account of divine intervention. One of the most revered Vishnu temples in Odisha.
